I see this, no intro sentences.... "Understanding your gap map Gaps in core technical skills are the most important to address. If a skill appears in most postings for your target role and you have little to no experience with it, that's a genuine gap worth closing before you apply widely. The question is whether you can close it quickly enough, or whether it changes your targeting. Gaps in nice-to-have technical skills are less critical. If you're missing something that only appears occasionally in postings, or that's listed as a bonus rather than a requirement, it doesn't need to block your search. Note it, but don't let it slow you down. Gaps in transferable skills are often not real gaps at all. They're usually a framing problem. You have the experience, but you haven't connected it explicitly to the new role. The fix isn't more experience, it's better positioning. The CV and LinkedIn sections of this programme cover this directly. Gaps in soft skills are worth taking seriously but are rarely dealbreakers at the application stage. They matter more at interview. If you know presenting findings or explaining technical work clearly is a weakness, that's worth working on before you get to interviews, not necessarily before you apply."

13:44

Section 4 – Mapping Your Skills (Preview)
Interactive artifact ∙ Version 3 

That should now show the intro paragraph before the gap categories. Can you see it updated on your end?

"

## Transferable skills: going deeper

Because transferable skills are rarely listed explicitly in job postings, candidates tend to underestimate how much weight they carry in hiring decisions, particularly at the junior level where technical differentiation between candidates is small.

Hiring managers at this level are often making a bet on potential and trajectory, not just current capability. Transferable skills are a significant part of what that bet is based on.

Here are the transferable skills that carry most weight in tech roles, with prompts to help you assess whether you have genuine evidence for them.

**Communication and explanation.** Can you explain a technical concept to someone without a technical background? Have you written documentation, presented findings, or trained colleagues? Evidence can come from any professional or educational context.

**Structured problem-solving.** Do you have examples of breaking down a complex problem, identifying what you needed to find out, working through it systematically, and arriving at a solution? This is different from just "solving problems." The structure matters.

**Working with ambiguity.** Have you made decisions or produced work when the brief was unclear, the data was incomplete, or the requirements changed mid-project? This is common in tech roles and valued more than most candidates expect.

**Ownership and follow-through.** Do you have examples of taking something from start to finish without being managed step by step? Side projects, freelance work, and self-initiated learning all count here.

**Collaboration and handling feedback.** Have you worked in teams where you had to manage conflicting views, incorporate others' input, or give feedback constructively? Pair programming, code review, and group projects are all relevant evidence.

Don't list every transferable skill you've ever had. Focus on the ones most relevant to your target role and where your evidence is strongest."

i know you said we cover this earlier but not as in the level of detail we have here so just wondering if it's worth it to stillhave a more dedicated section towards the top

13:52

It's a fair question. The earlier dropdowns cover the concept of transferable skills and how to find non-obvious ones, but they don't do what this new content does: give a concrete taxonomy of the specific transferable skills that actually matter in tech hiring, with prompts for each.

That's genuinely different and useful. The earlier content says "here's how to think about transferable skills." This says "here are the five that hiring managers actually care about, go check your evidence."

My recommendation: add it as a dedicated section between "The three categories that matter" and "What counts as evidence." That positioning makes sense logically — you've introduced the three categories, then you go deeper on the one most candidates underhandle, then you move into evidence. It also means learners hit it before the mapping exercise, which is where they need it.

Want me to add it there? I can drop it in as a <details> expandable to keep the page from feeling heavy, or as a full section if you think it earns that. Given the level of detail and how much it changes what people actually do in the checkpoint, I'd lean towards a full section.

yes

ok look, we need to streamline the ocntent because we have this: 

---

### How to spot transferable skills that aren't obvious

The mistake most people make is looking for direct task overlap: *"I did X in my old job, and X appears in the job posting."* But **transferable skills usually live one level below the task.** They're the underlying capability that made you good at the task, not the task itself.

**Example:** A chef moving into AI engineering didn't write code in a professional kitchen. But they almost certainly developed the ability to work accurately under time pressure, manage multiple parallel processes without losing track, make fast decisions with incomplete information, and maintain rigorous standards when fatigue and stress make it easy to cut corners.

Those aren't soft generalities. Those are specific, demonstrable capabilities that map directly onto what technical roles actually require.

The question to ask isn't *"did I do anything similar?"* It's *"what did I get consistently good at, and where does that matter in the role I'm targeting?"*

Some prompts to help you find non-obvious transferable skills:

- What decisions did you make regularly that other people found difficult?
- Where were you the person others came to when something went wrong?
- What processes did you own, improve, or build from scratch?
- Where did you have to explain complex things to people who didn't share your context?
- When did you have to deliver results despite unclear instructions, missing resources, or changing requirements?

Any honest answer to those questions is a transferable skill. The framing for your job search comes later. For now, just find them.

and then this:

Transferable skills: going deeper Because transferable skills are rarely listed explicitly in job postings, candidates tend to underestimate how much weight they carry in hiring decisions, particularly at the junior level where technical differentiation between candidates is small. Hiring managers at this level are often making a bet on potential and trajectory, not just current capability. Transferable skills are a significant part of what that bet is based on. Below are the transferable skills that carry most weight in tech roles, with prompts to help you assess whether you have genuine evidence for each one. Communication and explanation. Can you explain a technical concept to someone without a technical background? Have you written documentation, presented findings, or trained colleagues? Evidence can come from any professional or educational context. Structured problem-solving. Do you have examples of breaking down a complex problem, identifying what you needed to find out, working through it systematically, and arriving at a solution? This is different from just "solving problems." The structure matters. Working with ambiguity. Have you made decisions or produced work when the brief was unclear, the data was incomplete, or the requirements changed mid-project? This is common in tech roles and valued more than most candidates expect. Ownership and follow-through. Do you have examples of taking something from start to finish without being managed step by step? Side projects, freelance work, and self-initiated learning all count here. Collaboration and handling feedback. Have you worked in teams where you had to manage conflicting views, incorporate others' input, or give feedback constructively? Pair programming, code review, and group projects are all relevant evidence. Don't try to evidence every transferable skill you've ever had. Focus on the ones most relevant to your target role and where your evidence is strongest. Two or three well-evidenced transferable skills are more convincing than a long list of vague ones.

Section 4 – Mapping Your Skills (Preview)
Interactive artifact ∙ Version 6 

Listo. Ahora la estructura es:

  1. The three categories that matter — definiciones limpias de las tres, con transferable skills definida brevemente al final.
  2. How to spot transferable skills that aren't obvious — sección completa que abre con el "why it matters", sigue con el concepto "one level below the task" y el ejemplo del chef, y luego pasa directamente a las cinco skills con sus evidence prompts y el callout de cierre.

Sin repeticiones, sin overlap, orden lógico.

## The three categories that matter

When you map your skills against a target role, you're working across three categories. All three matter, and most candidates underweight at least one.

- **Technical skills** are the tools, technologies, and domain knowledge the role requires. For a Data Analyst this might mean SQL, Python, and Power BI. For an AI Engineer it might mean LangChain, RAG pipelines, and API integration. These are the skills most visible in job postings and most scrutinised in technical assessments.

- **Transferable skills** are skills you've built in previous roles, projects, or contexts that apply directly to your target role, even if they weren't acquired in a tech context. The obvious examples are things like project management experience transferring to any coordination-heavy role, or a finance background transferring to financial analytics. But transferability often runs deeper than that, and most career switchers miss it.

- **Soft skills** are the professional capabilities that shape how you work, not just what you produce. The ones that actually carry weight in hiring decisions tend to be more specific than the generic terms that appear in most job postings. Critical thinking, AI and digital literacy, persuasive communication, result orientation, focus and self-management, strategic adaptability, and a demonstrated commitment to continuous learning are all skills that hiring managers look for but rarely see evidenced well. 
  
   > 🧭 **One thing worth noting**
   >
  > Soft skills are weighted differently depending on where you are in the hiring process. At the application stage, they're rarely the deciding factor: most screening decisions are made on technical fit. But by the interview stage, they often become the primary differentiator between candidates with similar technical profiles. That's worth knowing now, because it changes how you prepare. You don't need to lead with soft skills in your CV. You do need to have specific, rehearsed examples ready before you get to interview.

![Skills categories](https://i.imgur.com/KQuUEaO.jpg)

---

### How to spot transferable skills that aren't obvious

The mistake most people make is looking for direct task overlap: *"I did X in my old job, and X appears in the job posting."* But **transferable skills usually live one level below the task.** They're the underlying capability that made you good at the task, not the task itself.

**Example:** A chef moving into AI engineering didn't write code in a professional kitchen. But they almost certainly developed the ability to work accurately under time pressure, manage multiple parallel processes without losing track, make fast decisions with incomplete information, and maintain rigorous standards when fatigue and stress make it easy to cut corners.

![Transferrable Skills](https://i.imgur.com/wBMH2xX.jpg)

Those aren't soft generalities. Those are specific, demonstrable capabilities that map directly onto what technical roles actually require.

The question to ask isn't *"did I do anything similar?"* It's *"what did I get consistently good at, and where does that matter in the role I'm targeting?"*

Some prompts to help you find non-obvious transferable skills:

- What decisions did you make regularly that other people found difficult?
- Where were you the person others came to when something went wrong?
- What processes did you own, improve, or build from scratch?
- Where did you have to explain complex things to people who didn't share your context?
- When did you have to deliver results despite unclear instructions, missing resources, or changing requirements?

Any honest answer to those questions is a transferable skill. The framing for your job search comes later. For now, just find them.

---

## Transferable skills: going deeper

Because transferable skills are rarely listed explicitly in job postings, candidates tend to underestimate how much weight they carry in hiring decisions, particularly at the junior level where technical differentiation between candidates is small.

Hiring managers at this level are often making a bet on potential and trajectory, not just current capability. Transferable skills are a significant part of what that bet is based on.

Here are the transferable skills that carry most weight in tech roles, with prompts to help you assess whether you have genuine evidence for them.

**Communication and explanation.** Can you explain a technical concept to someone without a technical background? Have you written documentation, presented findings, or trained colleagues? Evidence can come from any professional or educational context.

**Structured problem-solving.** Do you have examples of breaking down a complex problem, identifying what you needed to find out, working through it systematically, and arriving at a solution? This is different from just "solving problems." The structure matters.

**Working with ambiguity.** Have you made decisions or produced work when the brief was unclear, the data was incomplete, or the requirements changed mid-project? This is common in tech roles and valued more than most candidates expect.

**Ownership and follow-through.** Do you have examples of taking something from start to finish without being managed step by step? Side projects, freelance work, and self-initiated learning all count here.

**Collaboration and handling feedback.** Have you worked in teams where you had to manage conflicting views, incorporate others' input, or give feedback constructively? Pair programming, code review, and group projects are all relevant evidence.

Don't list every transferable skill you've ever had. Focus on the ones most relevant to your target role and where your evidence is strongest.

do you feel the content here overlaps and or should be merged/streamlined? 

## Understanding your gap map

Not every gap carries the same weight. Before you decide what to do about yours, it helps to know what kind of gap you're actually dealing with.

- **Gaps in core technical skills** are the most important to address. If a skill appears in most postings for your target role and you have little to no experience with it, that's a genuine gap worth closing before you apply widely. The question is whether you can close it quickly enough, or whether it changes your targeting.

- **Gaps in nice-to-have technical skills** are less critical. If you're missing something that only appears occasionally in postings, or that's listed as a bonus rather than a requirement, it doesn't need to block your search. Note it, but don't let it slow you down.

- **Gaps in transferable skills** are often not real gaps at all. They're usually a framing problem. You have the experience, but you haven't connected it explicitly to the new role. The fix isn't more experience, it's better positioning. 

- **Gaps in soft skills** are worth taking seriously but are rarely dealbreakers at the application stage. They matter more at interview. If you know presenting findings or explaining technical work clearly is a weakness, that's worth working on before you get to interviews, not necessarily before you apply.

---

## What to do with your gaps

Not all gaps need to be closed before you apply. Here's a framework for deciding what to do with each one.

- **Close it now** if it's a core technical requirement that appears in most postings for your target role and you can realistically strengthen it in a few weeks. Common examples: getting comfortable with a specific tool you've only used briefly, or completing a small project to demonstrate a skill you have theoretically but haven't applied yet.

- **Apply anyway and address it directly** if the gap is in a nice-to-have, or you have a strong differentiator that compensates for it. In your cover letter or in interviews, you can acknowledge the gap and explain how your other experience makes you a strong candidate despite it. This works far better than hoping nobody notices.

- **Reconsider your target** if you're missing multiple core requirements and closing them would take months rather than weeks. This isn't failure, it's useful information. It might mean targeting a slightly different role that's a better fit for where you are now, or adjusting your seniority level.

- **Use it as your story** if the gap is actually a transition narrative. You're moving from one field to another and you're building new skills alongside bringing existing ones. That story, told well, is genuinely compelling to the right employers. 

  <details>
  <summary><strong>What to do if your gap list feels overwhelming</strong></summary>
   
   It's common to reach this step and feel like there's too much to fix before you can start applying. A few things worth remembering:
   
   - You don't need to close every gap before you apply. Employers hiring at junior and entry level expect candidates to continue developing on the job. A long list of gaps isn't unusual and isn't disqualifying.
   
   - Prioritise two or three skills: the ones that appear most frequently in your target role and where you have the most ground to cover. Treat everything else as secondary until those are addressed.
   
   - A very long gap list sometimes signals a targeting problem rather than a skills problem. If the distance between your current profile and your target role is large across most of the critical skills, it may be worth going back to the previous section and asking whether there's a more realistic entry point.
   
   </details>
14:32

Yes, there's meaningful overlap. Both sections are essentially answering the same question: "what do I do with my gaps?" The first categorises them by type and tells you how seriously to take each one. The second gives you a decision framework for acting on them. But the categories in the first map almost directly onto the actions in the second:

  • Core technical gap → Close it now
  • Nice-to-have gap → Apply anyway
  • Transferable gap → framing problem (positioning)
  • Soft skill gap → matters at interview, not application stage

So a reader goes through the first section, then immediately reads the second section and thinks "yes, I know, you just told me this."

My recommendation: merge them into one section. Keep "Understanding your gap map" as the title, open with the intro sentence, then for each gap type give both the diagnosis and the action in the same breath. The dropdown stays at the end. That cuts the repetition and makes it more useful because the "what it is" and "what to do about it" live together.

Want me to do it?

The dropdown stays at the end as a pressure valve for people who feel overwhelmed. No redundant section header, no repeated framing.

rgba(0,0,0,0.06);margin-bottom:24px;} .picker-card h1{font-family:'DM Serif Display',serif;font-size:2rem;margin-bottom:12px;line-height:1.2;} .picker-card .lead{font-size:0.93rem;color:#555;line-height:1.75;margin-bottom:32px;font-weight:300;} .mode-grid{display:grid;grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr;gap:14px;} .mode-btn{background:#fafafa;border:1.5px solid #e0ddd6;border-radius:14px;padding:24px 22px;text-align:left;cursor:pointer;font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;transition:all 0.2s;display:flex;flex-direction:column;gap:10px;justify-content:space-between;} .mode-btn .mode-top{display:flex;flex-direction:column;gap:10px;flex:1;} .mode-btn:hover{border-color:#1a8e87;background:#f0faf9;transform:translateY(-2px);box-shadow:0 4px 16px rgba(26,142,135,0.1);} .mode-icon{font-size:1.5rem;line-height:1;} .mode-label{font-size:1rem;font-weight:500;color:#1a1a1a;line-height:1.3;} .mode-desc{font-size:0.8rem;color:#888;line-height:1.55;font-weight:300;} .mode-tag{display:inline-block;font-size:0.65rem;font-weight:600;letter-spacing:0.07em;text-transform:uppercase;padding:3px 8px;border-radius:99px;margin-top:4px;} .tag-a{background:#f0faf9;color:#1a8e87;border:0.5px solid #1a8e87;} .tag-b{background:#fff0f4;color:#ca4365;border:0.5px solid #ca4365;} .section-header{text-align:center;margin-bottom:24px;} .section-tag{display:inline-block;font-size:0.72rem;font-weight:600;letter-spacing:0.1em;text-transform:uppercase;color:#999;margin-bottom:10px;} .section-title{font-family:'DM Serif Display',serif;font-size:1.9rem;color:#1a1a1a;line-height:1.25;margin-bottom:10px;} .section-sub{font-size:0.88rem;color:#777;font-weight:300;max-width:520px;margin:0 auto;line-height:1.65;} .instr-box{background:#fff;border-radius:10px;padding:14px 16px;box-shadow:0 1px 6px rgba(0,0,0,0.05);margin-bottom:16px;border-left:3px solid #1a8e87;} .instr-box p{font-size:0.82rem;color:#444;line-height:1.65;} .instr-box p+p{margin-top:6px;} .instr-box strong{font-weight:500;color:#1a1a1a;} .instr-box.mode-b{border-left-color:#ca4365;} .skills-list{display:flex;flex-direction:column;gap:10px;margin-bottom:16px;} .skill-card{background:#fff;border-radius:12px;padding:18px 20px;box-shadow:0 1px 6px rgba(0,0,0,0.05);border:2px solid transparent;transition:border-color 0.2s;} .skill-card.s-confident{border-color:#1a8e87;} .skill-card.s-developing{border-color:#d79053;} .skill-card.s-gap{border-color:#ca4365;} .skill-top{display:flex;align-items:flex-start;gap:10px;margin-bottom:10px;} .skill-name-wrap{flex:1;} .skill-name{font-size:0.93rem;font-weight:500;color:#1a1a1a;margin-bottom:2px;} .skill-desc{font-size:0.76rem;color:#aaa;line-height:1.45;} .badge{font-size:0.63rem;font-weight:600;letter-spacing:0.06em;text-transform:uppercase;padding:3px 8px;border-radius:99px;white-space:nowrap;flex-shrink:0;margin-top:2px;} .b-core{background:#fff0f4;color:#ca4365;border:0.5px solid #ca4365;} .b-nice{background:#f0faf9;color:#1a8e87;border:0.5px solid #1a8e87;} .del-btn{background:none;border:none;color:#ccc;cursor:pointer;font-size:12px;padding:2px;border-radius:4px;flex-shrink:0;} .del-btn:hover{color:#ca4365;} .status-btns{display:flex;gap:6px;margin-bottom:0;} .sk-btn{flex:1;padding:7px 4px;border-radius:8px;border:1.5px solid #e0e0e1;background:#fafafa;font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;font-size:0.76rem;cursor:pointer;color:#888;transition:all 0.17s;text-align:center;} .sk-btn:hover{border-color:#bbb;color:#444;} .sk-btn.on-confident{background:#1a8e87;border-color:#1a8e87;color:#fff;font-weight:500;} .sk-btn.on-developing{background:#d79053;border-color:#d79053;color:#fff;font-weight:500;} .sk-btn.on-gap{background:#ca4365;border-color:#ca4365;color:#fff;font-weight:500;} .skill-detail{display:none;flex-direction:column;gap:8px;margin-top:10px;} .skill-detail.open{display:flex;} .ev-area{width:100%;border:0.5px solid #e0e0e1;border-radius:8px;padding:8px 10px;font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;font-size:0.77rem;color:#1a1a1a;background:#fafafa;resize:none;min-height:38px;line-height:1.5;transition:border-color 0.15s;} .ev-area:focus{outline:none;border-color:#1a8e87;background:#fff;} .ev-area::placeholder{color:#c0c2c8;font-style:italic;} .gap-priority{background:#fff8f9;border:0.5px solid #f5c0cc;border-radius:8px;padding:12px 14px;display:flex;flex-direction:column;gap:10px;} .gp-label{font-size:0.7rem;font-weight:600;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:0.07em;color:#ca4365;margin-bottom:2px;} .gp-row{display:flex;align-items:flex-start;gap:10px;} .gp-q{font-size:0.79rem;color:#6b1a2e;line-height:1.5;flex:1;padding-top:2px;} .gp-opts{display:flex;gap:5px;flex-wrap:wrap;} .gp-btn{padding:5px 10px;border-radius:99px;border:1px solid #e8c0c8;background:#fff;font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;font-size:0.72rem;cursor:pointer;color:#8b2a3e;transition:all 0.15s;white-space:nowrap;} .gp-btn:hover{border-color:#ca4365;background:#fff0f4;} .gp-btn.gp-on{background:#ca4365;border-color:#ca4365;color:#fff;font-weight:500;} .add-area{background:#fff;border-radius:12px;padding:14px 16px;box-shadow:0 1px 4px rgba(0,0,0,0.04);margin-bottom:20px;border:1.5px dashed #d4cfc6;} .add-label{font-size:0.72rem;font-weight:500;color:#aaa;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:0.07em;margin-bottom:9px;} .add-row{display:flex;gap:7px;margin-bottom:6px;flex-wrap:wrap;} .add-input{flex:1;min-width:150px;border:0.5px solid #ddd;border-radius:8px;padding:8px 11px;font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;font-size:0.83rem;background:#fafafa;color:#1a1a1a;} .add-input:focus{outline:none;border-color:#1a8e87;} .add-sel{border:0.5px solid #ddd;border-radius:8px;padding:8px 9px;font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;font-size:0.8rem;background:#fafafa;color:#1a1a1a;cursor:pointer;} .add-btn{background:#1a8e87;color:#fff;border:none;border-radius:8px;padding:8px 15px;font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;font-size:0.82rem;cursor:pointer;} .add-btn:hover{background:#147a74;} .add-sub{width:100%;border:0.5px solid #ddd;border-radius:8px;padding:7px 11px;font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;font-size:0.78rem;background:#fafafa;color:#1a1a1a;} .add-sub:focus{outline:none;border-color:#1a8e87;} .nav-row{display:flex;align-items:center;justify-content:space-between;margin-top:4px;} .back-btn{background:none;border:none;font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;font-size:0.83rem;color:#aaa;cursor:pointer;padding:8px 0;display:flex;align-items:center;gap:5px;transition:color 0.2s;} .back-btn:hover{color:#555;} .next-btn{background:#1a1a1a;color:#fff;border:none;border-radius:100px;padding:12px 28px;font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;font-size:0.88rem;cursor:pointer;transition:background 0.2s;} .next-btn:hover{background:#333;} .result-card{background:#1a1a1a;border-radius:16px;padding:48px 40px;color:#fff;box-shadow:0 4px 24px rgba(0,0,0,0.15);margin-bottom:16px;} .r-tag{display:inline-block;font-size:0.68rem;font-weight:600;letter-spacing:0.12em;text-transform:uppercase;padding:5px 13px;border-radius:100px;margin-bottom:18px;} .r-tag-a{background:#1a8e87;color:#fff;} .r-tag-b{background:#ca4365;color:#fff;} .r-title{font-family:'DM Serif Display',serif;font-size:2rem;margin-bottom:10px;line-height:1.2;} .r-desc{font-size:0.85rem;line-height:1.7;color:#888;margin-bottom:24px;} .stats-row{display:grid;grid-template-columns:repeat(3,1fr);gap:8px;margin-bottom:28px;} .stat-box{background:rgba(255,255,255,0.06);border-radius:10px;padding:14px 10px;text-align:center;} .stat-n{font-size:1.6rem;font-weight:500;display:block;line-height:1;} .stat-l{font-size:0.65rem;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:0.06em;color:#666;margin-top:5px;} .c-conf{color:#4ecfbb;}.c-dev{color:#f0a86a;}.c-gap{color:#e06080;} .r-section{margin-bottom:22px;} .r-sec-head{font-size:0.68rem;font-weight:600;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:0.08em;color:#555;margin-bottom:10px;padding-bottom:7px;border-bottom:0.5px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.07);} .r-item{display:flex;align-items:flex-start;gap:10px;padding:9px 0;border-bottom:0.5px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.05);} .r-item:last-child{border-bottom:none;} .r-dot{width:6px;height:6px;border-radius:50%;flex-shrink:0;margin-top:6px;} .d-conf{background:#1a8e87;}.d-dev{background:#d79053;}.d-gap{background:#ca4365;} .r-body{flex:1;} .r-name{font-size:0.85rem;color:#ddd;line-height:1.4;} .r-ev{font-size:0.74rem;color:#666;margin-top:3px;line-height:1.4;} .r-tags{display:flex;gap:4px;flex-shrink:0;flex-wrap:wrap;justify-content:flex-end;max-width:170px;} .rtag{font-size:0.6rem;padding:2px 7px;border-radius:99px;font-weight:600;white-space:nowrap;} .rt-core{background:rgba(202,67,101,0.25);color:#f0a0b5;} .rt-nice{background:rgba(26,142,135,0.2);color:#5ecfc5;} .rt-close{background:rgba(26,142,135,0.28);color:#5ecfc5;} .rt-apply{background:rgba(128,140,244,0.28);color:#b0b8ff;} .rt-reconsider{background:rgba(202,67,101,0.32);color:#f0a0b5;} .rt-story{background:rgba(215,144,83,0.25);color:#f0c090;} .rt-unprioritised{background:rgba(255,255,255,0.08);color:#777;} .r-actions{display:flex;gap:10px;margin-top:26px;flex-wrap:wrap;} .export-btn{background:#1a8e87;color:#fff;border:none;border-radius:100px;padding:12px 24px;font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;font-size:0.86rem;cursor:pointer;transition:background 0.2s;} .export-btn:hover{background:#147a74;} .restart-btn{background:transparent;border:1.5px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.18);color:#fff;font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;font-size:0.86rem;padding:12px 24px;border-radius:100px;cursor:pointer;transition:all 0.2s;} .restart-btn:hover{background:rgba(255,255,255,0.07);border-color:rgba(255,255,255,0.35);} .unassessed-note{font-size:0.77rem;color:#555;padding:12px 14px;background:rgba(255,255,255,0.04);border-radius:8px;line-height:1.55;margin-top:4px;} .action-legend{margin-top:20px;padding:14px 16px;background:rgba(255,255,255,0.04);border-radius:8px;} .legend-head{font-size:0.68rem;font-weight:600;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:0.08em;color:#555;margin-bottom:10px;} .legend-items{display:flex;flex-direction:column;gap:6px;} .legend-item{display:flex;align-items:baseline;gap:8px;font-size:0.78rem;color:#888;line-height:1.5;} .legend-item .rtag{flex-shrink:0;} @media(max-width:560px){ .picker-card,.result-card{padding:26px 20px;} .mode-grid{grid-template-columns:1fr;} .section-title{font-size:1.5rem;} .gp-row{flex-direction:column;gap:6px;} } </style> </head> <body> <div class="container"> <div class="progress" id="prog"> <div class="pd active" id="d0"></div> <div class="pd" id="d1"></div> <div class="pd" id="d2"></div> <div class="pd" id="d3"></div> <div class="pd" id="d4"></div> </div> <!-- MODE PICKER --> <div class="step active" id="s-pick"> <div class="picker-card"> <h1>Map your skills</h1> <p class="lead">Choose how you want to use this tool. Both modes walk you through the same three skill categories. The difference is what you do with your gaps.</p> <div class="mode-grid"> <button class="mode-btn" onclick="startMode('A')"> <div class="mode-top"> <div class="mode-icon">📋</div> <div class="mode-label">Build your skills inventory</div> <div class="mode-desc">Map everything you have across technical, transferable, and soft skills. Mark your status and add evidence for each one. No scoring — just an honest picture of where you stand.</div> </div> <span class="mode-tag tag-a">General inventory</span> </button> <button class="mode-btn" onclick="startMode('B')"> <div class="mode-top"> <div class="mode-icon">🎯</div> <div class="mode-label">Analyse your skills gaps</div> <div class="mode-desc">Same inventory, but for each gap you also answer two questions: how often did this skill appear in your job posting research, and how long would it realistically take you to close it? You get a prioritised gap list with suggested actions.</div> </div> <span class="mode-tag tag-b">Gap analysis</span> </button> </div> </div> </div> <!-- TECHNICAL --> <div class="step" id="s-tech"> <div class="section-header"> <div class="section-tag" id="tag-tech">Technical skills</div> <div class="section-title">Technical skills</div> <div class="section-sub">Tools, languages, and domain knowledge from your job posting research.</div> </div> <div class="instr-box" id="instr-tech"> <p><strong>Step 1 of this section:</strong> add every technical skill that appeared in your job posting research — core requirements and nice-to-haves. The examples marked * are drawn from common AE and AIB postings. Replace or delete them and add your own.</p> <p><strong>Step 2:</strong> for each skill, mark your status and add a short evidence note. Be specific — "used Python in my capstone to clean and analyse a 50,000-row dataset" is useful; "know Python" is not.</p> </div> <div class="skills-list" id="l-tech"></div> <div class="add-area"> <div class="add-label">Add a technical skill from your research</div> <div class="add-row"> <input class="add-input" id="n-tech-name" type="text" placeholder="Skill name"> <select class="add-sel" id="n-tech-imp"><option value="core">core</option><option value="nice">nice-to-have</option></select> <button class="add-btn" onclick="addSkill('tech')">+ Add</button> </div> <input class="add-sub" id="n-tech-sub" type="text" placeholder="Optional: how often it appeared, or brief context"> </div> <div class="nav-row"> <button class="back-btn" onclick="go('s-pick',0)">← Back</button> <button class="next-btn" onclick="go('s-trans',2)">Transferable skills →</button> </div> </div> <!-- TRANSFERABLE --> <div class="step" id="s-trans"> <div class="section-header"> <div class="section-tag" id="tag-trans">Transferable skills</div> <div class="section-title">Transferable skills</div> <div class="section-sub">Skills from previous roles or contexts that apply to your target role, even if they weren't acquired in a tech setting.</div> </div> <div class="instr-box" id="instr-trans"> <p><strong>Where to look:</strong> previous work, academic, or voluntary experience. Include anything that's genuinely relevant — project management, client communication, domain expertise, data work in non-tech roles.</p> <p>Be generous here. Career switchers consistently underestimate how much weight transferable skills carry at junior level, where technical differentiation between candidates is often small.</p> </div> <div class="skills-list" id="l-trans"></div> <div class="add-area"> <div class="add-label">Add a transferable skill from your background</div> <div class="add-row"> <input class="add-input" id="n-trans-name" type="text" placeholder="Skill name"> <select class="add-sel" id="n-trans-imp"><option value="core">core</option><option value="nice">nice-to-have</option></select> <button class="add-btn" onclick="addSkill('trans')">+ Add</button> </div> <input class="add-sub" id="n-trans-sub" type="text" placeholder="Optional: where this appeared in your research or why it's relevant"> </div> <div class="nav-row"> <button class="back-btn" onclick="go('s-tech',1)">← Back</button> <button class="next-btn" onclick="go('s-soft',3)">Soft skills →</button> </div> </div> <!-- SOFT --> <div class="step" id="s-soft"> <div class="section-header"> <div class="section-tag" id="tag-soft">Soft skills</div> <div class="section-title">Soft skills</div> <div class="section-sub">The interpersonal and professional skills that determine how you work, not just what you can do.</div> </div> <div class="instr-box" id="instr-soft"> <p><strong>Only add what you can evidence.</strong> "Good communicator" tells a recruiter nothing. "Presented findings from my capstone to a non-technical audience and rewrote the summary based on their feedback" tells them something real.</p> <p>For each skill you add, write the specific example in the evidence field. You'll use these directly in interviews.</p> </div> <div class="skills-list" id="l-soft"></div> <div class="add-area"> <div class="add-label">Add a soft skill you can evidence</div> <div class="add-row"> <input class="add-input" id="n-soft-name" type="text" placeholder="Skill name"> <select class="add-sel" id="n-soft-imp"><option value="core">core</option><option value="nice">nice-to-have</option></select> <button class="add-btn" onclick="addSkill('soft')">+ Add</button> </div> <input class="add-sub" id="n-soft-sub" type="text" placeholder="Optional: brief description"> </div> <div class="nav-row"> <button class="back-btn" onclick="go('s-trans',2)">← Back</button> <button class="next-btn" onclick="showResults()">See my results →</button> </div> </div> <!-- RESULTS --> <div class="step" id="s-results"> <div class="result-card"> <div class="r-tag" id="r-mode-tag">Your skills map</div> <div class="r-title" id="r-title">Inventory summary</div> <div class="r-desc" id="r-desc"></div> <div class="stats-row"> <div class="stat-box"><span class="stat-n c-conf" id="cnt-conf">0</span><span class="stat-l">Confident</span></div> <div class="stat-box"><span class="stat-n c-dev" id="cnt-dev">0</span><span class="stat-l">Developing</span></div> <div class="stat-box"><span class="stat-n c-gap" id="cnt-gap">0</span><span class="stat-l">Gap</span></div> </div> <div id="sec-conf" class="r-section" style="display:none;"> <div class="r-sec-head">Confident — lead with these in every application</div> <div id="list-conf"></div> </div> <div id="sec-dev" class="r-section" style="display:none;"> <div class="r-sec-head">Developing — be specific about where you are; don't just claim them</div> <div id="list-dev"></div> </div> <div id="sec-gap" class="r-section" style="display:none;"> <div class="r-sec-head" id="gap-sec-head">Gaps</div> <div id="list-gap"></div> </div> <div id="action-legend" class="action-legend" style="display:none;"> <div class="legend-head">What the suggested actions mean</div> <div class="legend-items"> <div class="legend-item"><span class="rtag rt-close">Close it now</span>Core requirement, appears frequently, closable during the programme. Address it before applying widely.</div> <div class="legend-item"><span class="rtag rt-story">Use as your story</span>You're building this skill as part of your transition. That narrative, told well, is compelling to the right employers.</div> <div class="legend-item"><span class="rtag rt-apply">Apply anyway</span>Nice-to-have or rare requirement. Acknowledge it directly in applications rather than waiting to close it.</div> <div class="legend-item"><span class="rtag rt-reconsider">Revisit targeting</span>Core requirement, appears in most postings, not easily closable. Worth asking whether there's a more realistic entry point first.</div> <div class="legend-item"><span class="rtag rt-unprioritised">Not prioritised</span>You didn't answer the gap questions for this skill. Go back to add frequency and closability if you want a suggested action.</div> </div> </div> <div id="note-unassessed" class="unassessed-note" style="display:none;"></div> <div class="r-actions"> <button class="export-btn" onclick="exportMap()">Export my skills map</button> <button class="restart-btn" onclick="go('s-pick',0)">↩ Start again</button> </div> </div> <div style="text-align:center;margin-top:4px;"> <button class="back-btn" onclick="go('s-soft',3)" style="display:inline-flex;">← Back to soft skills</button> </div> </div> </div> <script> let MODE='A'; const ST={tech:[],trans:[],soft:[]}; const DEFS={ tech:[ {name:'Python',desc:'AE: scripting, automation, AI applications — AIB: automating workflows, working with APIs',imp:'core',eg:true}, {name:'LangChain / LLM frameworks',desc:'AE: building LLM-powered applications, RAG pipelines, agents — appears in most AE postings',imp:'core',eg:true}, {name:'API integration',desc:'AE: connecting to OpenAI and third-party services — AIB: using AI tools via API in business workflows',imp:'core',eg:true}, {name:'Prompt engineering',desc:'AE and AIB: designing, testing, and iterating on prompts for real outputs',imp:'core',eg:true}, {name:'Vector databases',desc:'AE: Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma — common in RAG-focused roles',imp:'nice',eg:true}, {name:'No-code / low-code AI tools',desc:'AIB: Make, Zapier, n8n — common in operations and automation roles',imp:'nice',eg:true}, ], trans:[ {name:'Process or workflow analysis',desc:'AIB especially: mapping how work currently happens before automating it',imp:'core',eg:true}, {name:'Explaining technical concepts to non-technical people',desc:'AE and AIB: translating what an AI system does into plain language for stakeholders',imp:'core',eg:true}, {name:'Domain or industry knowledge',desc:"Any sector expertise from previous work — can be a strong differentiator if it matches the company's industry",imp:'nice',eg:true}, ], soft:[ {name:'Working with ambiguity',desc:'AI roles often involve unclear briefs, changing requirements, and incomplete information',imp:'core',eg:true}, {name:'Structured problem-solving',desc:'Breaking a complex problem down, working through it systematically, arriving at something defensible',imp:'core',eg:true}, {name:'Receiving and acting on feedback',desc:'Relevant in any role with peer review, iteration, or cross-team work',imp:'nice',eg:true}, ] }; function blank(s){return{...s,status:null,evidence:'',freq:null,closable:null};} function startMode(m){ MODE=m; ST.tech=[];ST.trans=[];ST.soft=[]; document.querySelectorAll('.instr-box').forEach(el=>{el.classList.toggle('mode-b',m==='B');}); const prefix=m==='B'?'Gap analysis · ':'Inventory · '; document.getElementById('tag-tech').textContent=prefix+'Technical skills'; document.getElementById('tag-trans').textContent=prefix+'Transferable skills'; document.getElementById('tag-soft').textContent=prefix+'Soft skills'; const instrTech=document.getElementById('instr-tech'); const existingBNote=instrTech.querySelector('.b-note'); if(m==='B'&&!existingBNote){ const note=document.createElement('p'); note.className='b-note';note.style.marginTop='6px'; note.innerHTML='<strong>Gap analysis mode:</strong> when you mark a skill as Gap, two extra questions appear below it — how often it appeared in your research, and whether you can close it during the programme. Your answers determine the suggested action in your results.'; instrTech.appendChild(note); }else if(m==='A'&&existingBNote){existingBNote.remove();} go('s-tech',1); } function initSec(sec){if(!ST[sec].length)ST[sec]=DEFS[sec].map(blank);render(sec);} function render(sec){ const c=document.getElementById('l-'+sec);c.innerHTML=''; ST[sec].forEach((sk,i)=>{const d=document.createElement('div');d.className='skill-card'+(sk.status?' s-'+sk.status:'');d.innerHTML=cardHTML(sk,sec,i);c.appendChild(d);}); } function cardHTML(sk,sec,i){ const detOpen=sk.status?' open':''; const showGP=MODE==='B'&&sk.status==='gap'; return( '<div class="skill-top">'+ '<div class="skill-name-wrap">'+ '<div class="skill-name">'+x(sk.name)+(sk.eg?' <span style="color:#d79053;font-weight:400">*</span>':'')+'</div>'+ (sk.desc?'<div class="skill-desc">'+x(sk.desc)+'</div>':'')+ '</div>'+ '<span class="badge '+(sk.imp==='core'?'b-core':'b-nice')+'">'+(sk.imp==='core'?'core':'nice-to-have')+'</span>'+ '<button class="del-btn" onclick="del(\''+sec+'\','+i+')">✕</button>'+ '</div>'+ '<div class="status-btns">'+sBtn(sk,'confident','✓ Confident',sec,i)+sBtn(sk,'developing','~ Developing',sec,i)+sBtn(sk,'gap','✗ Gap',sec,i)+'</div>'+ '<div class="skill-detail'+detOpen+'">'+ '<textarea class="ev-area" placeholder="Evidence: which project, role, or context shows this? One specific sentence is enough." onchange="save(\''+sec+'\','+i+',\'evidence\',this.value)">'+x(sk.evidence||'')+'</textarea>'+ (MODE==='B'? '<div class="gap-priority" id="gp-'+sec+'-'+i+'" style="'+(showGP?'':'display:none')+'">'+ '<div class="gp-label">Gap priority questions</div>'+ '<div class="gp-row"><div class="gp-q">How often did this skill appear in your job posting research?</div>'+ '<div class="gp-opts">'+gBtn(sk,'freq','most','Most postings',sec,i)+gBtn(sk,'freq','some','Some postings',sec,i)+gBtn(sk,'freq','few','A few postings',sec,i)+'</div></div>'+ '<div class="gp-row"><div class="gp-q">How long would it realistically take you to close this gap?</div>'+ '<div class="gp-opts">'+gBtn(sk,'closable','yes','A few weeks',sec,i)+gBtn(sk,'closable','maybe','A few months',sec,i)+gBtn(sk,'closable','no','Longer than that',sec,i)+'</div></div>'+ '</div>' :'')+ '</div>' ); } function sBtn(sk,val,lbl,sec,i){return'<button class="sk-btn'+(sk.status===val?' on-'+val:'')+'" onclick="setStatus(\''+sec+'\','+i+',\''+val+'\',this)">'+lbl+'</button>';} function gBtn(sk,field,val,lbl,sec,i){return'<button class="gp-btn'+(sk[field]===val?' gp-on':'')+'" onclick="setGP(\''+sec+'\','+i+',\''+field+'\',\''+val+'\',this)">'+lbl+'</button>';} function setStatus(sec,i,val,btn){ ST[sec][i].status=val; if(val!=='gap'){ST[sec][i].freq=null;ST[sec][i].closable=null;} const card=btn.closest('.skill-card'); card.className='skill-card s-'+val; card.querySelectorAll('.sk-btn').forEach(b=>b.classList.remove('on-confident','on-developing','on-gap')); btn.classList.add('on-'+val); card.querySelector('.skill-detail').classList.add('open'); if(MODE==='B'){const gp=document.getElementById('gp-'+sec+'-'+i);if(gp)gp.style.display=val==='gap'?'flex':'none';} } function setGP(sec,i,field,val,btn){ST[sec][i][field]=val;btn.closest('.gp-opts').querySelectorAll('.gp-btn').forEach(b=>b.classList.remove('gp-on'));btn.classList.add('gp-on');} function save(sec,i,field,val){ST[sec][i][field]=val;} function del(sec,i){ST[sec].splice(i,1);render(sec);} function addSkill(sec){ const nEl=document.getElementById('n-'+sec+'-name'),sEl=document.getElementById('n-'+sec+'-sub'),iEl=document.getElementById('n-'+sec+'-imp'); const name=nEl.value.trim();if(!name)return; ST[sec].push({name,desc:sEl.value.trim(),imp:iEl.value,eg:false,status:null,evidence:'',freq:null,closable:null}); render(sec);nEl.value='';sEl.value=''; } function go(id,depth){ document.querySelectorAll('.step').forEach(s=>s.classList.remove('active')); document.getElementById(id).classList.add('active'); const isRes=id==='s-results',isPick=id==='s-pick'; document.getElementById('prog').style.display=(isRes||isPick)?'none':'flex'; if(!isRes&&!isPick){document.querySelectorAll('.pd').forEach((d,i)=>{d.classList.remove('active','done');if(i<depth)d.classList.add('done');else if(i===depth)d.classList.add('active');});} if(id==='s-tech')initSec('tech'); if(id==='s-trans')initSec('trans'); if(id==='s-soft')initSec('soft'); window.scrollTo({top:0,behavior:'smooth'}); } function gapAction(sk){ const{freq,closable,imp}=sk; if(!freq||!closable)return null; if(imp==='core'&&freq==='most'&&closable==='no')return'reconsider'; if((freq==='most'||freq==='some')&&(closable==='yes'||closable==='maybe'))return'close'; if(imp==='core'&&(freq==='some'||freq==='few')&&closable==='no')return'story'; if(imp==='nice'||freq==='few')return'apply'; return'apply'; } const ACTION_ORDER={reconsider:0,close:1,story:2,apply:3,null:4}; const ACTION_META={ close:{label:'Close it now',cls:'rt-close'}, apply:{label:'Apply anyway',cls:'rt-apply'}, reconsider:{label:'Revisit targeting',cls:'rt-reconsider'}, story:{label:'Use as your story',cls:'rt-story'}, }; function showResults(){ let nConf=0,nDev=0,nGap=0,nU=0; const conf=[],dev=[],gaps=[]; ['tech','trans','soft'].forEach(sec=>{ ST[sec].forEach(sk=>{ if(!sk.status){nU++;return;} const o={...sk,action:MODE==='B'&&sk.status==='gap'?gapAction(sk):null}; if(sk.status==='confident'){nConf++;conf.push(o);} else if(sk.status==='developing'){nDev++;dev.push(o);} else{nGap++;gaps.push(o);} }); }); if(MODE==='B')gaps.sort((a,b)=>{ const ao=ACTION_ORDER[a.action]??4,bo=ACTION_ORDER[b.action]??4; if(ao!==bo)return ao-bo; return(a.imp==='core'?0:1)-(b.imp==='core'?0:1); }); document.getElementById('cnt-conf').textContent=nConf; document.getElementById('cnt-dev').textContent=nDev; document.getElementById('cnt-gap').textContent=nGap; const isB=MODE==='B'; document.getElementById('r-mode-tag').className='r-tag '+(isB?'r-tag-b':'r-tag-a'); document.getElementById('r-mode-tag').textContent=isB?'Gap analysis':'Skills inventory'; document.getElementById('r-title').textContent=isB?'Gap analysis':'Inventory summary'; document.getElementById('r-desc').textContent=isB ?'Your gaps are sorted by suggested action, then by core vs nice-to-have. Each action is based on how often the skill appeared in your research and whether you can close it during the programme.' :'A structured view of what you have and where the gaps are. Use this when writing your CV, preparing for interviews, and deciding which applications are worth your time.'; document.getElementById('gap-sec-head').textContent=isB?'Gaps — prioritised with suggested actions':'Gaps — skills to address before or during your search'; document.getElementById('action-legend').style.display=isB?'block':'none'; renderList('conf',conf,false); renderList('dev',dev,false); renderList('gap',gaps,isB); const uEl=document.getElementById('note-unassessed'); uEl.style.display=nU>0?'block':'none'; if(nU>0)uEl.textContent=nU+' skill'+(nU>1?'s were':' was')+' not assessed. Go back and mark '+(nU>1?'them':'it')+' if you want a complete picture.'; go('s-results',5); } function renderList(type,items,showAction){ const sec=document.getElementById('sec-'+type),list=document.getElementById('list-'+type); if(!items.length){sec.style.display='none';return;} sec.style.display='block'; const dotCls=type==='conf'?'d-conf':type==='dev'?'d-dev':'d-gap'; list.innerHTML=items.map(sk=>{ let tags='<span class="rtag '+(sk.imp==='core'?'rt-core':'rt-nice')+'">'+(sk.imp==='core'?'core':'nice-to-have')+'</span>'; if(showAction){const m=sk.action?ACTION_META[sk.action]:{label:'Not prioritised',cls:'rt-unprioritised'};tags+='<span class="rtag '+m.cls+'">'+m.label+'</span>';} return'<div class="r-item"><div class="r-dot '+dotCls+'"></div><div class="r-body"><div class="r-name">'+x(sk.name)+'</div>'+(sk.evidence?'<div class="r-ev">'+x(sk.evidence)+'</div>':'')+'</div><div class="r-tags">'+tags+'</div></div>'; }).join(''); } function exportMap(){ const AMAP={close:'Close it now',apply:'Apply anyway',reconsider:'Revisit targeting',story:'Use as your story'}; const FMAP={most:'Most postings',some:'Some postings',few:'A few postings'}; const CMAP={yes:'Yes',maybe:'Maybe',no:'Needs outside work'}; let out='MY SKILLS MAP\n'+'='.repeat(50)+'\nMode: '+(MODE==='B'?'Gap analysis':'General inventory')+'\n\n'; ['tech','trans','soft'].forEach(sec=>{ out+=(sec==='tech'?'TECHNICAL':sec==='trans'?'TRANSFERABLE':'SOFT')+' SKILLS\n'+'-'.repeat(30)+'\n'; ST[sec].forEach(sk=>{ const st=sk.status?sk.status.charAt(0).toUpperCase()+sk.status.slice(1):'Not assessed'; out+='\n'+sk.name+' ['+(sk.imp==='core'?'CORE':'nice-to-have')+']\nStatus: '+st+'\nEvidence: '+(sk.evidence||'None added')+'\n'; if(MODE==='B'&&sk.status==='gap'){ out+='Frequency in research: '+(FMAP[sk.freq]||'Not answered')+'\n'; out+='Time to close: '+(CMAP[sk.closable]||'Not answered')+'\n'; out+='Suggested action: '+(sk.action?AMAP[sk.action]:'Not prioritised — answer the gap questions to get a suggested action')+'\n'; } }); out+='\n'; }); const a=document.createElement('a'); a.href=URL.createObjectURL(new Blob([out],{type:'text/plain'})); a.download='my-skills-map.txt';a.click(); } function x(s){return String(s).replace(/&/g,'&amp;').replace(/</g,'&lt;').replace(/>/g,'&gt;').replace(/"/g,'&quot;');} </script> </body> </html>
